segments.s032-g17: که is the relative 'that/who' introducing a subordinate clause. پرورده is a past participle 'reared/raised' used predicatively (literally 'reared'), followed by the agent بت‌پرستان 'by idolaters' (no ezāfe in this poetic line). بدند is an archaic/plural past form of بودن 'to be' meaning 'they were'. The clause thus says 'who were reared by idolaters.'
segments.s032-g18: سرآسیمه is an adverb/adjectival participle meaning 'distraught/utterly agitated'. بر سان is a fixed poetic pattern meaning 'in the manner of / like' (بر + سان + noun). مستان is the plural noun 'drunkards/ecstatics'. Again بدند (they were) closes the clause — 'they were distraught, like drunkards.'
segments.s032-g19: پس is a discourse particle 'then/thereupon'. آن دختران is the definite/topic-marked phrase 'those maidens'; جهاندار is an epithet 'world-holder/sovereign' modifying جم (Jam), the king's name — together 'those maidens of Jam the world-ruler.'
segments.s032-g20: به marks the indirect/benefactive relationship 'to/onto.' نرگس گل سرخ را is the definite object 'the red narcissus-flower' (را is the object marker). داده نم literally 'gave dew' — a poetic way to say 'sprinkled/let fall dew upon' (داده = given; نم = dew). The verb phrase at the end follows classical poetic word order (object before verb).
segments.s032-g21: گشادند here is used as a verbal/nominalized form 'opened/uttered' (they opened speech). بر introduces the addressee 'to/toward Afarīdūn', and سخن is the noun 'speech/words' — together 'they opened speech to Afarīdūn' (i.e., they spoke to him).
segments.s032-g22: که introduces reported speech 'that'. تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; باش is an optative/imperative 'be' (here 'may you be/remain'). تا functions temporally 'until/so long as', هست is 'exists/is', and گیتی کهن 'the ancient world' is the temporal frame — so the clause means 'May you be as long as the ancient world exists.'
segments.s032-g23: چه is an exclamative 'what' and اختر is 'star' used metaphorically; بد modifies اختر as 'ill/bad' (ill-omened). این 'this' and از تو 'from/of you' indicate provenance or association, and ای نیک‌بخت is a vocative address 'O fortunate one' — the whole is a rhetorical question: 'What an ill-starred thing is this of yours, O lucky one?'
segments.s032-g24: چه here is again interrogative 'what' and باری is an exclamatory/elliptical particle (classical usage 'now/then/what now'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', شاخ 'branch/bough', کدامین 'which', and درخت 'tree' — yielding the question 'What now? From the branch of which tree?' (asking the origin/source).
segments.s032-g81: Poetic word order: 'از این کشور' is a prepositional phrase ('from this country'). The two verbs 'آید' and 'شود' frame motion/change — here both are third-person present/future forms, so the clause means roughly 'he will come/leave and go to another (place)'. 'به دیگر' = 'to another (place)'; the subject is omitted and recovered from context.
segments.s032-g82: 'ز' is the poetic (short) form of 'از' meaning 'from' or 'because of'. 'رنج' is the suffering/pain; 'دو مار سیه' is the noun phrase 'two black snakes' (adjective after noun). 'نغنود' is an archaic/poetic present/future third-person form 'he/it falls asleep; he slumbers' (the subject again is implicit). Together: 'from the pain of the two black snakes he will fall asleep.'
segments.s032-g83: 'بیامد' here is a narrative perfect/past (or present-with-past-force) meaning 'has come' or 'came'; 'کنون گاه' = 'now the time/occasion'. 'بازآمدنش' is a deverbal noun with the third-person suffix '-ش' meaning 'his return'. Word order is verb + temporal phrase + noun (poetic): 'now the time of his return has come.'
segments.s032-g84: The conjunction 'که' introduces a result/reason clause ('for/that'). 'جایی' literally 'any place/somewhere'; 'نباید' is impersonal modal here ('there ought not to be / there will be no'); 'فراوان بدنش' strings an adverb/adjective ('plentiful/whole') with 'بدنش' ('his body'). The sense is idiomatic: 'there will be no place where his body can be plentiful/whole' i.e. 'there will be nowhere his body will be intact/at rest.'
segments.s032-g85: 'گشاد' is used poetically as 'open/revealed' (an adjectival/participial use from 'گشودن'); 'آن نگار' = 'that beloved' is the subject. 'جگر خسته راز' groups as 'the secret (راز) of the weary heart' (جگر = heart/seat of feeling, خسته = tired). Word order places the participle before the subject and the object phrase after it, typical of Ferdowsi's elevated diction.
segments.s032-g86: 'نهاده' is a perfect/participial form meaning 'placed' or 'put'. 'بدو' is the dative 'to him'; 'گوش' here is used idiomatically as 'an ear' i.e. 'attention/listening'; 'گردن‌فراز' is an epithet 'the proud / haughty-necked one' describing the doer. The clause literally says '(he) placed to him an ear, the proud one' — idiomatically 'the haughty one bent his neck and listened.'
segments.s032-g33: چنین (an adverb) modifies the following verb: it means 'thus/so'. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (here 'gave an answer'); پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. فریدون is the subject (name). که is the conjunction/relative introducing the subordinate clause that follows ('that...'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: literally 'Thus gave answer Fereydun that...'.
segments.s032-g34: نماند is the past negative sense 'did not remain / does not last' (here 'does not endure'). به کس is a dative phrase 'for anyone' (به + کس). جاودانه is an adverb/adjective 'eternal/everlasting' modifying the idea of lasting. نه بخت coordinates a second item ('nor fortune'); نه negates the following noun and links it to the preceding clause, giving the sense 'neither throne nor fortune lasts forever for anyone.'
segments.s032-g35: منم is emphatic 'I am'. پور means 'son/descendant' and here marks a genitive relationship without an overt preposition (common in Persian: پورِ آبتین = 'son of Abtin'). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. نیک‌بخت is an epithet 'fortunate' applied to the name that follows; آبتین is the proper name (the ancestor).
segments.s032-g36: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whom/that' modifying آبتین. بگرفت is the simple past 'seized / took' (بَ + گرفت in classical/poetic usage). ضحاک is the subject/agent of the verb. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and ایران‌زمین is the object of that preposition ('the land of Iran'). The object of بگرفت (آبتین) is carried over from the main clause, so the relative clause means 'whom Zahhak seized from the land of Iran.'
segments.s032-g37: بکشتش is past-tense 'he killed him' with the third-person object suffix -ش ('him'). به زاری is an adverbial phrase 'with/in lamentation' (به + زاری). و is the coordinating 'and'. من is the pronoun 'I'. کینه‌جوی is a compound adjective/noun 'grudge-seeking' or 'vengeful/seeking revenge' (کینه + جوی, where جوی here functions as 'seeker/of ...-seeking'). Together this clause sets up the speaker's motive.
segments.s032-g38: نهادم is the first-person past of نهادن 'to place/set' (here idiomatically 'I set/turned'). سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. تخت ضحاک is a genitive phrase 'Zahhak's throne' (noun + possessor). روی is the noun 'face' used idiomatically with نهادم ('I set my face toward' = 'I turned toward'). The clause means 'I turned my face toward Zahhak's throne.'
segments.s032-g39: همان 'that/same' identifies the noun. گاو is 'cow'. برمایه is a compound meaning 'source of wealth/sustenance' (بر + مایه). که‌م here is که + enclitic ـم (my), forming a relative 'that was my ...' so the relative clause follows: دایه بود = 'was a nurse'. Thus the phrase reads 'that cow, my nurse and source of sustenance.'
segments.s032-g40: ز is the preposition 'from/of' (poetic). پیکر تنش is a double-expression of 'body/figure' with the possessive suffix -ش 'his/its' ('his body/figure'). همچو means 'like/as'. پیرایه is 'ornament/adornment'. بود is the past copula 'was'. Syntactically the clause says (of that cow) 'from its body/figure it was like an ornament', i.e. it appeared as an ornament on his body—poetic word order and ellipsis are at work.
segments.s032-g57: فریدون is the subject (proper name). چنین modifies پاسخ and means “thus/so.” پاسخ is the noun “answer/reply.” آورد is the past verb “brought/uttered” (here = ‘gave/said’); باز is an adverb “again” placed after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s032-g58: که‌گر is the archaic conditional “که گر” = “if.” چرخ here is a poetic image for the wheel of fate (the cycle of fortune). دادم is first‑person past “I caused/put (it) in motion” and works with دهد (third‑person) to form the conditional/optative (“if I turn/caused to turn … may it give …”). دهد is a third‑person present/optative “may/does give.” از فراز is a postpositional phrase “from on high.”
segments.s032-g59: ببرم is a volitional/subjunctive first‑person form expressing intent (“I will take/go after”). پی governs the following noun and here means “in pursuit of/after.” اژدها را is the definite object “the dragon,” and ز خاک gives the origin/location “from the earth/ground.”
segments.s032-g60: بشویم is a subjunctive/optative form meaning roughly “let it become / I will make (it) be” in this context (i.e., effecting cleansing). جهان را is the definite object “the world.” ز ناپاک پاک is a two‑part phrase meaning “from unclean to clean” (ز marks the source/state removed; پاک is the resulting state “pure/clean”).
segments.s032-g61: بباید is the modal “must/ought” and introduces obligation; شما را is the addressee marked by the object/topic marker را (“to you”). کنون is the adverb “now.” گفت here is the action ‘to say/tell’ used in this construction (the listed senses do not include the verb sense, so it is serving as the lexical ‘say/tell’), and راست is the direct object/adverbial “the truth/truly.”
segments.s032-g62: که introduces the subordinate clause (“that/which/where”). آن بی‑بها is a demonstrative plus adjective “that priceless.” اژدهافش is an epithet applied to the referent (here rendered as a formidable/dragon‑like figure). کجاست is the fused interrogative + copula (کجا + ست) meaning “where is …?” — the copula ست marks existence/location in this poetic word order.
segments.s032-g63: بر او is a prepositional phrase “to/for him” (بر here functions toward/at). خوب‑رویان is a plural subject “the fair‑faced ones” (those of fair face). گشادند is a past plural verb form meaning “opened/revealed” (poetic form of گشودن) and takes راز “secret” as its object; the verb appears after subject in this line’s word order. راز is the noun “secret.”
segments.s032-g64: مگر introduces an exception “unless.” کاژدها را is the dragon marked with را. سر آید is a verbal phrase (سر آمدن/آوردن) here meaning “the head comes/should come” — idiomatically “the head is brought to / comes to be ….” به گاز is an instrumental phrase “by/through a bite,” so the whole clause means roughly “unless the dragon’s head should come to a bite” (i.e., be bitten off).
segments.s032-g49: "بدو گفت شاه آفریدون تویی" is ordered like: indirect object + verb + subject + predicate. بدو (to him) is a prepositional/adverbial element placed before the verb گفت (said); شاه is the grammatical subject 'the king'; آفریدون تویی is the predicate (آفریدون = name, تویی = 'you are'). تویی is the 2nd-person present copula (thou art) used as a predicate.
segments.s032-g50: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that...'. ویران here functions as the verbal/adjectival root 'ruin/devastate' describing what the named person does; کنی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive form of کردن used here as a verbal auxiliary meaning 'you do/you cause' (= 'you destroy'). تنبل و جادویی are coordinated nouns/adjectives 'the lazy and the magical/sorcerous (ones)'; the clause as a whole modifies آفریدون ('who destroys the lazy and sorcerers').
segments.s032-g51: This is a rhetorical question plus a verbless locative statement. کجا 'where' begins the question; هوش ضحاک 'Zahhāk's wit' is the topic; بر دست تست is a locative phrase meaning 'is on your hand/at your command' with تست functioning as an emphatic/old 2nd‑person copula (here meaning roughly 'is yours' or 'is in your hand'). Word order places the locative phrase after the topic for emphasis.
segments.s032-g52: گشاد here is a past/participial form meaning 'opened' (classical/poetic usage), followed by the object جهان 'the world'. بر کمربست is a causative/resultative expression 'he/she/you fastened/girded to the belt' (کمربست = 'girded, bound to the belt'). The final تست again functions as an old 2nd‑person copula/ending linking these actions to 'you' (i.e. 'you opened the world and girded it to your belt').
segments.s032-g53: This noun phrase z + genitive gives origin: ز تخم کیان 'from the seed/stock of the Kayan (kings)'. ما دو 'we two' (the two of us) is the patient/topic; پوشیده پاک are two adjectives (hidden/covered, pure) describing that pair. Word order places the origin before the nominative 'we two' for poetic emphasis.
segments.s032-g54: شده رام is a passive/result phrase 'have become tame' (شده = become, رام = tame). با او marks the agent/means 'by him' or 'with him'; ز بیم هلاک is a prepositional phrase 'from/for fear of ruin' explaining the cause or motive. The whole clause says they have been rendered tame by him out of fear of destruction.
segments.s032-g55: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually' or 'still' modifying the verb خواند. جفتمان (from جف) literally 'our wrong/cruelty' appears as a noun; خواند here functions as 'called' or 'named' (classical use). او is the subject 'he'; جفت مار is the accusative/complement 'a pair of snakes' — the phrase he repeatedly named/applied to (us). The clause therefore reports that he kept calling (us) 'a pair of snakes'.
segments.s032-g56: This is a rhetorical question: چگونه 'how' + توان (modal auxiliary 'can') + بودن (infinitive 'to be') + ای (poetic/vocative or emphatic marker) + شهریار 'king'. Together they form 'How can you be a king?' with توان + بودن expressing ability/possibility in a classical phrase order.
segments.s032-g9: نهاد is a past‑tense verb (3rd sg.) meaning 'placed/put' and here forms the idiom پای نهاد = 'set foot'. از بر is a poetic prepositional sequence meaning 'from/off (the top of)'; تخت ضحاک is a genitive phrase 'Zahhak's throne'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: verb + prepositional phrase + object (foot).
segments.s032-g10: کلاه کئی = 'the Kayanian (royal) hat' — کلاه (noun) followed by the attributive کئی. جست is a simple past 'leapt/jumped', و is the coordinating 'and', and بگرفت is a past verb 'seized/took (hold of)'; جای is the object 'place/spot' (i.e. 'took its place'). The two verbs are sequential past actions.
segments.s032-g11: برون آورید is an imperative (2nd person plural) 'bring out/bring forth'. از + شبستان is 'from the bedchamber/inner chamber'; اوی is an archaic pronominal form meaning 'his' (here modifying شبستان). The order places the source phrase before the object for emphasis.
segments.s032-g12: بتان is the direct object 'idols' (plural). The two compounds سیه‌موی and خورشید‌روی are post‑nominal adjectival compounds ('dark‑haired' and 'sun‑faced') joined by و; Persian often places such adjectival compounds after the noun they modify.
segments.s032-g13: بفرمود is an honorific/perfective verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (polite narrative). It is followed by the infinitive شستن ('to wash') and the object سرانشان ('their heads' — here physical heads/hair, or leaders figuratively), with نخست 'first' indicating order. The construction expresses a command to have their heads washed first.
segments.s032-g14: روانشان 'their souls/spirits' is the object of بشست (past 'he washed'), with از آن تیرگی‌ها 'from those darknesses' marking what they are washed away from. The verb بشست is 3rd sg. past; the clause says he cleansed their inner being of the darkness (figurative ignorance/impurity).
segments.s032-g15: ره 'way/path' is the direct object; داور پاک is an epithet 'the pure/righteous Judge' (dāvar used as a title). بنمودشان is a past verb 'he showed them' (بنمود + -شان 'them'), so the phrase means 'the Judge showed them the pure way.' Note the noun object preceding the agentive epithet and verb — a common poetic ordering.
segments.s032-g16: ز آلودگی 'from pollution/defilement' marks the source or thing removed; پس is an adverb 'then/after that'; بپالودشان is a verbal form meaning 'he purified/cleansed them' (the verb پالودن with the pronominal suffix -شان). Together: 'then he cleansed them of pollution.'
segments.s032-g65: بگفتند is a classical past-reporting verb ('they said'); it introduces reported speech. کاو here is an archaic third‑person pronoun meaning 'he/that he' (not the verbal senses listed) and functions as the subject of the following clause. سوی + هندوستان is a noun phrase 'toward India' with سوی marking direction.
segments.s032-g66: بشد is a classical past/inchoative use of شدن ('he went/set out' / 'it came to pass') introducing the action; تا here marks purpose ('so that, in order that'). کند is the present/subjunctive form of کردن used to express the intended action ('may he do / he might make'). بند is a noun 'fetter / bond' (object of کند, i.e. 'to make a fetter'), and جادوستان is a poetic place-name 'land/place of sorcery' governed by بند.
segments.s032-g67: ببرد is the verbal form meaning 'he took / carried off' (classical narrative past). The following words form the direct object: سر 'head' (literal anatomical head) and بی‌گناهان هزار 'a thousand innocents' (بی‌گناهان = 'the innocent ones'; numeral follows the noun in Persian poetry).
segments.s032-g68: هراسان شده‌ست is a compound predicate: هراسان 'frightened' + شده‌ست 'has become/has been', giving 'has become frightened'. از introduces the cause ('because of'); بد روزگار is a genitive phrase meaning 'ill fortune / the bad times' (روزگار here = fate/times).
segments.s032-g69: کجا is an interrogative/rhetorical 'where / ever' here functioning as 'ever' in the sense of 'had ever'. گفته is the past participle/verb 'said/told'. بودش is the enclitic past auxiliary 'had been' or 'had been told to him' (بود + ش = 'it was to him' / 'he had been told'). یکی here means 'a certain / some' and پیشبین is 'seer / prophet', so together 'a certain seer'.
segments.s032-g70: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. پردختگی is a poetic noun here meaning something like 'ruination / finishing' (context: the land coming to an end), and گردد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of گشتن/گردیدن used to mean 'would become / would come to be'. از marks cause ('because of'), تو is the second‑person pronoun 'you', and زمین is 'the earth/land' — together 'that the land would be ruined because of you.'
segments.s032-g71: The first که begins the clause 'that', and آید is the present/subjunctive or future sense 'would come / will come'. The second که introduces the subordinate verb 'that (someone) seize', and گیرد is the present/subjunctive of گرفتن 'to seize/take' (here 'would seize'). سر تخت تو is the object phrase 'the head of your throne' (سر = 'head/top/lead' and تخت = 'throne'; تو = 'your').
segments.s032-g72: چگونه is 'how' (exclamatory). فرو is a prefix/adverb 'down / utterly', پژمرد is a past/stative verb 'to wither / to fade', and بخت تو is 'your fortune / fate' (bakt = luck/fate, تو = your). Together this is an exclamation about how completely your fortune will decline.
segments.s032-g73: This string contains two linked ideas: دلش زآن زده is a verbless clause meaning 'his heart, struck/from that' (زآن = زِ آن, 'because of that'; زده is a past‑participle/adjectival use meaning 'struck/affected'), and فال پر آتش است is a separate clause 'the omen is full of fire' with فال as subject, پر آتش as its predicate complement, and است the copula. The sequence is typical of Ferdowsi's terse, paratactic style where clauses sit side by side and the copula appears only where needed.
segments.s032-g74: همه زندگانی is the noun phrase 'the whole life' (همه as a distributive/totalizer modifying زندگانی). بر او functions like a dative/experiencer phrase 'to/for him' (بر + pronoun), and ناخوش است is a predicate 'is unhappy/unpleasant' with است the copula at the end. Word order places the predicate adjective before the copula, common in classical Persian.
segments.s032-g75: همی is a habitual/continuous marker meaning 'continually' or 'still'; خون is the direct object 'blood'; the coordinated list دام و دد و مرد و زن strings together categories (here 'cattle/animals, beasts/enemies, men and women'). This is a cataloging of victims/objects of the blood. Note: some classical nouns here are poetic/archaic.
segments.s032-g76: بریزد کند is a periphrastic habitual present: بریزد (3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive form of 'to pour') plus کند as an auxiliary giving iterative/continuous force 'keeps pouring'. در یکی آبدن is a prepositional phrase 'into one basin/cistern' (در + numeric determiner + place); the object of بریزد is understood as the blood mentioned earlier.
segments.s032-g77: مگر introduces an exception/conditional here: 'unless'. کاو is an archaic fused form (classical poetry) equivalent to 'که او' ('that he' / 'who'), introducing the subordinate clause. سر و تن is a coordinate object 'head and body', بشوید is a passive/reflexive sense 'be washed' (here conditioned), and به خون is the instrumental/means 'with/in blood'. The whole clause means 'unless he washes/washes off his head and body in blood'.
segments.s032-g78: شود is a 3rd‑person singular present/future/resultative verb here ('will become' / 'becomes') used after the conditional. فال اخترشناسان is a noun phrase where اخترشناسان ('the astrologers') qualifies فال ('omen') — classical Persian often allows noun+modifier without an explicit ezafe — and نگون is a postpositive adjective meaning 'ill‑fated/doomed'. Grammatically this states the result: 'the astrologers' omen will be ill‑fated.'
segments.s032-g79: همان نیز is an emphatic 'that same, too' or 'likewise'; از آن مارها is a genitive/partitive 'from those snakes'; بر ... کفت indicates location 'on (the) palms' (بر + noun), so the phrase means 'on both palms/from both palms' (کفت = palm). The line compresses the agent/source (snakes) and the location (palms) in classical, compact word order.
segments.s032-g80: به رنج دراز is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with/into a long suffering' (به marking the affected state), است مانده is a compound verbal idea: مانده (past participle 'remained') with the copula است ('has remained/is left'), and شگفت is a qualifying adjective 'astonishing' or 'marvelous' placed after the participle to color the remaining suffering. Together: 'a long, astonishing suffering has remained.'
segments.s032-g41: ز خون is a prepositional/poetic phrase 'from/of blood' placed before the noun phrase; چنان is an intensifier 'such' modifying the following noun phrase; بی‌زبان چارپای is a compound adjective + noun 'mute (bi‑zabān) quadruped' with the adjective before the head in this poetic ordering. The whole segment is a descriptive noun phrase that will be questioned by the following clause.
segments.s032-g42: چه آمد is an interrogative formula 'what came' where آمد is simple past (third person) used impersonally/'befell'; بر is the preposition 'upon/on'; آن مرد is 'that man' (آن determiner + noun) and ناپاک‌رای is a compound adjective 'evil‑minded' modifying مرد. Grammatically this is the main clause asking what befell that man, and it takes the preceding noun phrase as the thing that came upon him.
segments.s032-g43: کمر بسته‌ام is a perfective construction 'I have girded my waist/loins' with the past participle بسته plus the first‑person clitic ـَم. لاجرم is an adverb 'necessarily/inevitably' and جنگجوی 'warrior' follows without a copula — a typical Persian omission of the present copula, so the phrase reads as 'I am inevitably a warrior' or 'necessarily (I am) a warrior.'
segments.s032-g44: از ایران is a locative/source phrase 'from Iran'; به کین is purposive 'for/to (do) vengeance' (به + noun indicating purpose); اندر آورده is a compound verbal idea where آورده is a past participle 'brought/has come' and اندر gives the sense 'into/among' — together implying 'have come/been brought into (vengeance)'; روی ('face') here is literal 'face' used idiomatically to mean 'with (my) face directed toward' vengeance. The whole unit expresses motion/purpose: 'I have come from Iran for vengeance.'
segments.s032-g45: سرش را is the definite direct object 'his head' where ش is the clitic possessive and را is the object marker. بدین is an archaic/poetic contraction (به + این) meaning 'with this' (instrumental). گرزه is the noun 'mace' and گاو چهر is a compound modifier 'ox‑faced' describing the mace (گاو 'ox' + چهر 'face'). The instrumental phrase follows the object and precedes the verb, as is common in Persian poetic order.
segments.s032-g46: بکوبم is the present/subjunctive form (first person) of کوبیدن used here in a forceful, determinate sense 'I will strike/crush.' The coordination نه بخشایش آرم نه مهر uses نه ... نه ... 'neither ... nor ...'; بخشایش 'forgiveness' and مهر 'affection/kindness' are the two negated nouns, while آرم (archaic spelling/form related to آوردن here) functions as 'I will bring/grant' — together meaning 'I will grant neither forgiveness nor mercy.' This is a terse, emphatic vow.
segments.s032-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is simple past (third person) 'he heard'; از او 'from him' marks the source of the words; این سخن 'this speech/these words' is the object. ارنواز (a proper name) appears after the phrase — classical Persian often places the subject after the verb, so the clause reads 'When Ernawaz heard these words from him.'
segments.s032-g48: گشاده شدش is a passive/experiential construction 'was opened/became open for him' where گشاده 'open' + شدش ('it became' + clitic 'him') conveys that something opened up to him. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'; دل‌پاک is the compound adjective 'pure‑hearted' modifying راز 'secret'. Word order places the predicate first and the prepositional phrase after, yielding 'the secret became opened upon his pure heart.'
segments.s032-g1: This is a relative clause: 'طلسمی' (an enchantment/talisman) is modified by 'که ضحاک سازیده بود' — 'که' introduces the relative clause 'that Zahhak had made'. 'سازیده بود' is the compound past (pluperfect-like) formed from 'سازیــدن' + past auxiliary 'بود' (archaic epic past form here), meaning 'had made/constructed'.
segments.s032-g2: 'سرش' = 'its/his head' (possessive suffix 'ش' on 'سر'). Word order is subject/phrase then predicate: 'سرش به آسمان بر فرازیده بود' — 'had risen/been elevated toward the sky.' 'بر فرازیده بود' is an adjectival/predicative construction with past auxiliary 'بود' (archaic past) giving the perfect sense.
segments.s032-g3: 'فریدون ز بالا فرود آورید' = 'Fereydun brought (it) down from above.' 'ز' is the poetic/genitive 'from', 'بالا' = 'on high/above'. 'فرود آورد' is the verbal compound 'to bring down/descend' (here in archaic past form 'آورید' used for 3rd person past in epic style).
segments.s032-g4: 'که' introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason/observation: 'آن جز به نام جهاندار دید' = 'it saw nothing except the name "Jehandar".' 'آن' refers back to the thing just mentioned; 'جز' = 'except'; 'به نام' here means 'by/in the name (of)' and 'دید' is simple past 'saw.'
segments.s032-g5: 'و زآن جادوان کاندر ایوان بدند' starts with the connective 'و' (and). 'زآن جادوان' = 'from those magicians' or 'of those magicians'; 'کاندر' (ک + اندر) is an archaic fused relative/locative meaning 'who/which were in' or 'in which'; 'ایوان' is 'the hall/porch.' 'بدند' is an epic past/pluperfect form meaning 'were' (they existed/stood there).
segments.s032-g6: 'همه نامور نره دیوان بدند' predicatively describes those present: 'همه' = 'all', 'نامور' = 'renowned/famed', 'نره' = 'male/virile' (here an epithet), 'دیوان' refers to the demonic beings (Divs) in epic diction; 'بدند' again is the past copula 'they were.'
segments.s032-g7: 'سرانشان به گرز گران کرد پست' has object-first order: 'سرانشان' = 'their chiefs/leaders' (possessive suffix), followed by the instrument phrase 'به گرز گران' = 'with a heavy mace' (literally 'by heavy mace'), then the verb complex 'کرد پست' = 'made [them] low' = 'brought [them] down/struck them down.' 'کرد' is the simple past 'did/made' and 'پست' functions as the result/complement 'low/down.'
segments.s032-g8: 'نشست از بر گاه جادوپرست' follows as a resultant locative phrase: 'نشست' is used intransitively/predicatively ('sat/fell/settled'), 'از بر' = 'from off/upon', and 'گاه جادوپرست' = 'the sorcerer's seat/throne/place.' Together it conveys that they were made to fall from the sorcerer's seat. Note: 'گاه' here is a poetic 'place/seat' meaning rather than the common sense 'time'.
segments.s032-g25: که introduces this subordinate/exclamatory clause meaning 'that' or 'for/that now'; ایدون is an archaic adverb meaning 'now/at this moment' (poetic) and modifies the clause; به بالین is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the bedside' with بالین ('bedside') in the sense 'beside/near'; شیر is the possessed noun 'lion' and آمدی is the past-tense verb 'came' (used here in Ferdowsi's epic register to report a past arrival). Word order is typical Persian: subject (شیر/implicit) + verb final آمدی, with the prepositional phrase placed before the verb.
segments.s032-g26: This is a noun phrase in apposition followed by the same past verb آمدی: ستمکاره 'the oppressor' (a noun), مرد دلیر 'a brave/valiant man' (mرد modified by the adjective دلیر), and the repeated آمدی restates the arrival. The repetition is a poetic device that links the two apposed nouns to the single event 'came.'
segments.s032-g27: چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase 'what a source/what a cause'; جهان گشت is the verbal phrase 'the world turned/became' (گشت = 'became/turned'); بر ما is the dative/locative 'upon us'; and به بد is the predicate 'for the worse' ('به' + adjective بد). Overall: 'what a thing the world has become upon us — for the worse.'
segments.s032-g28: ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of/from'; کردار is 'deed, action'; این jads the demonstrative 'this' and is linked by ezāfe to جادوی 'of magic/sorcery' (جادوی = 'magic' with the genitive/ezāfe ending), while بی‌خرد post-modifies جادو to mean 'foolish magic'. Together: 'because of the deeds of this foolish magic.'
segments.s032-g29: ندیدیم کس is 'we saw no one / we have not seen anyone' (negated perfect/past). کاین چنین is a relative phrase 'who/that so' ('کاین' = 'that which/that' + چنین 'such'). زهره داشت literally 'had zohreh' — here زهره is an archaic poetic word meaning luck/fortune or favorable star (classical sense) and داشت is the past 'had/possessed'.
segments.s032-g30: بدین پایگه = 'in/at this station/position' (بدین = به + این, پایگه = station/base). از هنر بهره داشت is a possessive/partitive construction: از هنر 'of/from skill' + بهره 'a share/portion' + داشت 'he had' → 'he had a share of skill.' Word order places the locative phrase first, then the from‑phrase and the predicate.
segments.s032-g31: کش here is an older/poetic formative element (often intensive or part of a compound) attached to اندیشه to produce a sense like 'far-reaching or powerful thought'; گاه indicates 'at times' or 'on occasion'; او is the third-person pronoun 'his/he'; and آمدی is the past verb 'came/occurred.' In context the clause means roughly 'at times his powerful thought came to pass.'
segments.s032-g32: و is the conjunction 'and'; گرش is a noun meaning 'its turning/turn' (here 'when it turned'); آرزو is 'desire/wish' (the thing that turns); جاه او is 'his rank/honor' (جاه 'rank/status' modified by او); and آمدی again is the past 'came.' The clause reads 'and when his desire turned, his rank came' — a compact causal/resultive sequence.

sentences.s032-s9: Who had been reared by idolaters and ran about distraught, behaving like drunkards.
sentences.s032-s10: Then those maidens of Jam the world-ruler sprinkled dew upon the red narcissus flower.
sentences.s032-s11: They addressed Afarīdūn, saying, 'May you remain while the ancient world exists.'
sentences.s032-s12: What an ill-omened star is this of yours, O fortunate one? What then — from the branch of which tree?
sentences.s032-s41: He will go from this land to another; from the pain caused by the two black snakes he will fall asleep.
sentences.s032-s42: Now the time of his return has come, for there will be nowhere his body can be whole (or find rest).
sentences.s032-s43: That beloved revealed the secret of the weary heart and the haughty one bent his neck and lent him an attentive ear.
sentences.s032-s17: Fereydun thus replied that neither throne nor fortune remains forever for anyone.
sentences.s032-s18: I am the son of that fortunate Abtin whom Zahhak seized from the land of Iran.
sentences.s032-s19: He killed him amid lamentation, and I, seeking vengeance, set my face toward Zahhak's throne.
sentences.s032-s20: That same cow, my nurse and source of sustenance, was like an ornament upon his body.
sentences.s032-s29: Fereydun again replied thus: “If I set the wheel in motion, may it grant from on high.”
sentences.s032-s30: I will pursue the dragon from the earth, and I will cleanse the world — make it pure from impurity.
sentences.s032-s31: I ought now to tell you the truth: where is that priceless, dragon‑formidable one?
sentences.s032-s32: To him the fair‑faced ones disclosed the secret — unless the dragon’s head should meet a bite (i.e., be bitten off).
sentences.s032-s25: The king said to him, "You are Afrīdūn, who destroys the lazy and the sorcerers."
sentences.s032-s26: Where is Zahhāk's wit? It is in your hand; you opened the world and bound it to your girdle.
sentences.s032-s27: We two are of the pure, hidden seed of the Kayanian kings; we have been tamed by him for fear of destruction.
sentences.s032-s28: He kept calling us a pair of snakes; how can you be a king?
sentences.s032-s5: He set his foot down from Zahhak's throne; the Kayanian hat leapt up and seized its place.
sentences.s032-s6: Bring out of his bedchamber his idols, dark‑haired and sun‑faced.
sentences.s032-s7: He ordered that their heads be washed first; he washed their souls of those darknesses.
sentences.s032-s8: The righteous Judge showed them the pure way, then purified them of pollution.
sentences.s032-s33: They said that he went toward India so that he might make a barrier around the land of sorcery.
sentences.s032-s34: He carried off the heads of a thousand innocents; he has become terrified because of ill fortune.
sentences.s032-s35: Had any seer ever told (him) that the land would be ruined because of you?
sentences.s032-s36: That someone would come and seize the head of your throne — how completely your fortune would wither!
sentences.s032-s37: His heart, struck by that thing, is full of fire; his whole life is unhappy for him.
sentences.s032-s38: He keeps pouring blood — of cattle and beasts, of men and women — into a single basin.
sentences.s032-s39: Unless he wash his head and body in blood, the astrologers' omen will become ill‑fated.
sentences.s032-s40: Likewise, from those same snakes upon both palms a long and astonishing suffering remained.
sentences.s032-s21: What mute four‑footed beast, born of blood, came upon that evil‑minded man?
sentences.s032-s22: I have girded my loins; necessarily a warrior, I have come from Iran to seek vengeance.
sentences.s032-s23: I will crush his head with this ox‑faced mace; I will grant neither pardon nor pity.
sentences.s032-s24: When Ernawaz heard these words from him, the secret opened in his pure heart.
sentences.s032-s1: The enchantment that Zahhak had fashioned had raised its head up toward the sky.
sentences.s032-s2: Fereydun brought it down from on high, for it saw nothing except the name 'Jehandar'.
sentences.s032-s3: And from those magicians who were in the hall, all — the famed male divs — were there.
sentences.s032-s4: He struck down their chiefs with heavy maces; from the sorcerer's seat they were thrust down.
sentences.s032-s13: That now you have come to the lion's bedside; the oppressor — the brave man — has come.
sentences.s032-s14: How greatly the world has turned against us for the worse because of the deeds of this foolish magic.
sentences.s032-s15: We have seen no one who possessed such fortune; who in this station had any share of skill.
sentences.s032-s16: Sometimes his thinking came to pass, and when his desire turned, his rank arrived.
